description: The provided image is of extremely low resolution, and no scientific content related to the study of aging hardening behaviors of Fe-Mn-Al-Cu low-density steels is visually identifiable. Only the generic text 'Check for updates' is partially readable, with no microstructure images, mechanical performance curves, phase characterization results, or experimental data relevant to the studied Fe-35Mn-(15,17,19)Al-5Cu (at.%) alloys detected. No valid information supporting the research conclusions on Al content effects on β-Mn precipitation kinetics, hardness evolution, phase composition, or mechanical properties can be extracted from this visual content.

This is a schematic diagram illustrating the method for evaluating fracture toughness of Fe-Mn-Al-Cu low-density steels via indentation technique. The diagram marks the diamond-shaped Vickers indentation, radial cracks propagating from the four corners of the indentation, and the radial crack length $l_i$ measured from the indentation corner to the crack tip. It defines the parameter for fracture toughness calculation: the average crack length c is calculated as 0.25 times the sum of 4 $l_i$ values, with an indentation load of 9.8 N applied in the test. This schematic supports the fracture toughness characterization of three experimental alloys (15Al, 17Al, 19Al with elastic modulus of 164 GPa, 175 GPa, 181 GPa respectively), which verifies that increasing Al content reduces the fracture toughness of the investigated low-density steels from 8.6 MPa·m^(1/2) to 3.5 MPa·m^(1/2).

This figure displays Vickers hardness evolution and corresponding contour plots for three Fe-Mn-Al-Cu low-density steels (15Al, 17Al, 19Al) subjected to aging treatments at 525–650 °C for 0 to 10 h. Subplots a, b, and c correspond to 15Al, 17Al, and 19Al alloys respectively: each left line plot presents hardness as a function of aging time at six fixed aging temperatures, while each right contour plot maps hardness distribution across the full tested aging temperature-time space. All alloys exhibit hardness increase from ~250 HV (solution-treated state) to peak values with aging: peak hardness decreases as Al content rises, reaching ~966 HV for 15Al, ~925 HV for 17Al, and ~918 HV for 19Al. The optimal aging temperature window for peak hardness shifts to higher values with increasing Al content, matching the trend of suppressed β-Mn precipitation kinetics at higher Al levels. This figure confirms that Al content and aging parameters can be adjusted to obtain wide-range tunable hardness in the alloy system.

This figure presents Electron Backscatter Diffraction (EBSD) inverse pole figures (IPF, upper panel of each subplot) and corresponding phase maps (lower panel of each subplot) of three solution-treated Fe-Mn-Al-Cu low-density steels: (a) 15Al (Al atomic percent 15.0%) with a 500 μm scale bar, (b) 17Al (Al atomic percent 17.0%) with a 200 μm scale bar, and (c) 19Al (Al atomic percent 18.6%) with a 500 μm scale bar. The IPF uses a standard orientation color triangle mapped to <001>, <101>, <111> crystallographic directions. Phase maps confirm all three as-solutionized alloys contain only single α (Body-Centered Cubic, BCC) phase, with no γ (Face-Centered Cubic, FCC) phase detected. Average grain size decreases as Al content increases, with 15Al showing the coarsest grains and 19Al the finest. This figure verifies the single BCC initial microstructure of the solution-treated alloys, which acts as the matrix for β-Mn precipitation during subsequent aging treatment.

This figure presents Electron Backscatter Diffraction (EBSD) inverse pole figures and corresponding phase maps of 15Al Fe-Mn-Al-Cu low-density steel under different aging treatments. Subfigures a1 (low magnification, 100 μm scale) and a2 (high magnification, 5 μm scale) show the sample aged at 525 °C for 3 h, where massive β-Mn phase precipitates with a small amount of residual BCC α matrix. Subfigures b1 (200 μm scale) and b2 (5 μm scale) correspond to the sample aged at 550 °C for 6 h, where α matrix almost fully transforms to β-Mn, accompanied by fine new BCC precipitates inside β-Mn. Subfigures c1 (200 μm scale) and c2 (5 μm scale) show the sample aged at 650 °C for 3 h, where no residual α is detected and new FCC phase precipitates distribute in β-Mn matrix. The results demonstrate that aging parameters (temperature, duration) regulate phase evolution of the alloy, and β-Mn precipitation acts as the main origin of the strong aging hardening effect in this steel system.

This figure presents EBSD inverse pole figures (left columns a1, b1, c1) and corresponding phase maps (right columns a2, b2, c2, with low and high magnification views respectively) for the 17Al (Fe-34.8Mn-17Al-5Cu at.%) low-density steel under different aging conditions: a1/a2 correspond to aging at 525 °C for 10 h, showing 20.5 vol% β-Mn precipitated along BCC α matrix grain boundaries and inside grains with fine α-Mn particles; b1/b2 correspond to aging at 575 °C for 6 h, where α matrix nearly completely transforms to β-Mn with trace new FCC precipitates; c1/c2 correspond to aging at 650 °C for 10 h, where β-Mn matrix contains small new BCC precipitates. The results validate that elevated aging temperature promotes α to β-Mn phase transition, supporting that higher Al content reduces β-Mn precipitation kinetics in the studied steel system.

This group of figures presents EBSD inverse pole figures and phase maps of the 19Al (Fe-34.8Mn-18.6Al-5Cu at.%) low-density steel after different aging treatments. Subfigures a₁ and a₂ correspond to samples aged at 525 °C for 10 h, b₁ and b₂ correspond to samples aged at 600 °C for 3 h, c₁ and c₂ correspond to samples aged at 650 °C for 6 h, with a₂, b₂, c₂ being magnified views of a₁, b₁, c₁ respectively. Identified phases include BCC α matrix, α-Mn, β-Mn, and FCC precipitates. After aging at 525 °C for 10 h, only 12.1% β-Mn precipitates along grain boundaries and inside grains, with diverse grain orientations observed. After aging at 600 °C for 3 h, the α matrix almost fully transforms to β-Mn, with small amount of new FCC precipitates formed inside β-Mn. After aging at 650 °C for 6 h, the microstructure is nearly full β-Mn, with abundant new BCC precipitates distributed in β-Mn. This figure verifies that high Al content slows β-Mn precipitation kinetics, and phase evolution of 19Al alloy is strongly dependent on aging temperature.

This set of 16 secondary electron scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images presents the microstructure of the 15Al (Fe-35.2Mn-15Al-5.2Cu at.%) low-density steel after aging treatments at 525, 550, 575, 600, 625, and 650 °C for durations of 1 h, 3 h, 6 h, and 10 h, respectively. Each subfigure includes a low-magnification view with a 50 μm scale bar, and most include a high-magnification inset with a 1 μm or 2 μm scale bar. Fine particle-shaped α-Mn precipitates are observed in the early aging stage, while short rod-shaped β-Mn precipitates nucleate along α grain boundaries and inside grains, growing gradually with extended aging time or increased temperature. The α matrix is almost completely transformed to β-Mn after 3 h of aging at 525 °C, and new fine precipitates appear after longer aging or at higher aging temperatures. These observations confirm that β-Mn has rapid precipitation kinetics in the 15Al alloy, and the near-full phase transformation from α matrix to β-Mn is the origin of the achieved ultrahigh hardness of the aged alloy.

This set of secondary electron SEM images presents the microstructure evolution of 17Al Fe-Mn-Al-Cu low-density steel (Fe-34.8Mn-17Al-5Cu at.%) after different aging treatments, with aging temperatures ranging from 525 °C to 650 °C and aging durations of 1 h, 3 h, 6 h, 10 h, each sub-image is equipped with a 50 μm scale bar. Identified phases in the microstructures include β-Mn, α-Mn, and new fine precipitates. It can be observed that β-Mn precipitates first as aging proceeds, α-Mn forms at intermediate aging stages, while new nano-scale precipitates appear at higher aging temperatures (≥575 °C). The microstructure evolution revealed by this figure confirms that β-Mn precipitation kinetics in 17Al alloy is slower than that in lower Al content alloys, supporting the conclusion that increasing Al content suppresses β-Mn precipitation in Fe-Mn-Al-Cu low-density steels.

This figure presents secondary electron SEM images of the 19Al Fe-Mn-Al-Cu low-density steel (nominal composition Fe-34.8Mn-18.6Al-5Cu at.%) after various aging treatments, with aging temperatures ranging from 525 °C to 650 °C and durations of 1 h, 3 h, 6 h, 10 h. Each subgraph uses a 50 μm scale bar, and partial high-magnification insets are equipped with 10 μm scale bars. Characteristic phases including α-Mn, β-Mn, untransformed BCC α matrix, and new fine precipitates are marked and identified in the microstructures. With increasing aging temperature and extended duration, the precipitation and growth of Mn-rich phases and the consumption of initial α matrix are observed. These micrographs confirm that the 19Al alloy has the slowest β-Mn precipitation kinetics among the three investigated Fe-Mn-Al-Cu steels, supporting the conclusion that increased Al content suppresses β-Mn precipitation and shifts the optimal aging temperature for peak hardness to a higher range.

This is an X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of Fe-Mn-Al-Cu low-density steels, testing three samples: 15Al alloy aged at 550 °C for 1 h (black curve), 17Al alloy aged at 575 °C for 1 h (red curve), and 19Al alloy aged at 550 °C for 6 h (blue curve). The X-axis is 2θ ranging from 20° to 110°, and the Y-axis is diffraction intensity in arbitrary units. Diffraction peaks of three phases are labeled: α/D0₃ phase, α-Mn phase, and β-Mn phase. All three samples present coexisting α-Mn and β-Mn phases, verifying that α-Mn and β-Mn co-precipitate during aging before the BCC α matrix fully transforms to β-Mn. The tested aging conditions also reflect that higher Al content leads to slower β-Mn precipitation kinetics, so higher Al alloys require longer aging time or higher aging temperature to reach similar phase transformation levels.

This figure presents transmission electron microscopy (TEM) characterization results of the Fe-35Mn-15Al-5Cu (15Al) low-density steel after aging at 600 °C for 6 h. Subfigure (a) shows low-magnification bright-field and dark-field TEM images, revealing that the original α matrix fully transformed to abundant near-equiaxed β-Mn phases, with fine precipitates uniformly distributed at β-Mn interfaces. Subfigure (b) provides selected area electron diffraction (SAED) patterns of three marked regions, which confirm the crystal structures of β-Mn phase, FCC twin structure within precipitates, and FCC Cu-rich precipitates, respectively. Subfigure (c) displays corresponding EDS elemental mapping of Fe, Mn, Al, and Cu, verifying that the precipitates are Cu-rich, depleted in Fe and Mn, slightly poor in Al, while β-Mn is low in Cu. These results clarify phase compositions and distributions, supporting that the ultra-high hardness of the aged 15Al alloy is associated with massive β-Mn formation and dispersed Cu-rich precipitates.

This figure presents TEM characterization results of the 17Al Fe-Mn-Al-Cu low-density steel aged at 575 °C for 6 h. Subfigure (a) shows low-magnification bright-field (BF) TEM images revealing the overall precipitate morphology in the matrix. Subfigure (b) provides high-magnification BF TEM, corresponding EDS elemental mappings of Fe, Mn, Al, Cu (confirming Cu-rich precipitates, Mn-rich regions, and uniform Al/Fe distribution in the matrix), plus the SAED pattern of the blue circled region verifying FCC twin structure. Subfigure (c) displays BF TEM of β-Mn regions, with corresponding SAED pattern indexed under [111] beam and HRTEM image + FFT pattern confirming the β-Mn crystal structure. Subfigure (d) shows HRTEM of the interface between β-Mn and FCC Cu-rich phase, with FFT patterns for both phases. The results validate the coexistence of β-Mn, Cu-rich FCC precipitates and FCC twins in the aged 17Al alloy, supporting the aging hardening mechanism analysis for Fe-Mn-Al-Cu low-density steels.

This figure presents nanoindentation characterization results of three peak-aged Fe-Mn-Al-Cu low-density steels: 15Al (aged at 600 °C for 6 h), 17Al (aged at 575 °C for 6 h), and 19Al (aged at 600 °C for 10 h). Subplot a shows load-displacement nanoindentation curves for the three alloys, with measured average hardness values of 11.7±0.4 GPa for 15Al, 11.1±0.6 GPa for 17Al, and 10.4±1.3 GPa for 19Al, revealing that average hardness decreases while hardness deviation increases with rising Al content. The following three secondary electron microscopy images (with 2 μm scale bar) display the indent arrays on each sample surface, with corresponding point hardness values marked for each indent, verifying the wider hardness distribution in higher Al content alloys. The results support the conclusion that increasing Al content introduces larger amounts of soft FCC Cu-rich and BCC interfacial precipitates, which reduces overall alloy hardness despite a slight increase in intrinsic β-Mn phase hardness.

This bar chart displays the atomic percentage of Mn, Al, and Cu elements in β-Mn precipitates, measured via SEM-EDS, from the hardest (peak aged) samples of three Fe-Mn-Al-Cu low-density steel variants: 15Al, 17Al, and 19Al alloys. Mn content is nearly identical across the three samples, at 36.8 at.% for 15Al, 36.2 at.% for 17Al, and 36.2 at.% for 19Al. Al content in β-Mn increases with the nominal Al content of the bulk alloy, reaching 14.3 at.% for 15Al, 16.3 at.% for 17Al, and 17.5 at.% for 19Al. Cu content remains low and nearly unchanged, at 2.8 at.% for 15Al, 2.6 at.% for both 17Al and 19Al. These results support the conclusion that increased Al content raises the Al concentration dissolved in β-Mn, leading to slightly enhanced intrinsic hardness of β-Mn via solid solution strengthening, while Mn and Cu concentrations in β-Mn are not sensitive to nominal Al content variation.

This figure presents microstructural and compositional characterization results for three Fe-Mn-Al-Cu low-density steels with 15 at.%, 17 at.%, and 19 at.% Al content after respective aging treatment: 15Al aged at 525 °C for 3 h, 17Al and 19Al aged at 525 °C for 10 h. Each sample row includes four characterization outputs: Forescatter Detector (FSD) SEM images showing microstructure morphology, EBSD inverse pole figure (IPF) maps for grain orientation analysis, EBSD phase maps distinguishing β-Mn, BCC, α-Mn, and FCC phases, and EDS elemental maps for Fe, Mn, Al, Cu to visualize element partitioning. Observations confirm β-Mn is Mn-rich and depleted in Al and Cu, while higher Al content reduces β-Mn precipitation kinetics: 15Al exhibits near-complete transformation from α matrix to β-Mn after 3 h aging, while 17Al and 19Al only form partial, elongated β-Mn precipitates after 10 h at the same temperature. This supports the core conclusion that increasing Al content suppresses β-Mn phase transformation, which governs the aging hardening response of this alloy system.

This chart plots specific hardness (unit: HV·cm³/g) against density (unit: g/cm³) to compare the performance of developed Fe-Mn-Al-Cu low-density steels (15Al, 17Al, 19Al series, with Al atomic content increasing from 15% to 19%) with reference materials including other low-density steels, miscellaneous steels (stainless steel, high-speed steel, ferritic/martensitic steel, carbon steel, etc.), pure iron, and medium/high entropy alloys (M/HEAs). The developed steels show densities in the range of 6.66 to 6.85 g/cm³, with a maximum specific hardness exceeding 140 HV·cm³/g, which is significantly higher than the ~120 HV·cm³/g upper limit of the compared reference materials, and higher Al content corresponds to lower alloy density. This figure supports the core conclusion that aging-treated Fe-Mn-Al-Cu low-density steels realize ultra-high specific hardness, outperforming most existing structural metallic materials in this performance index.
